prompt: Give me concrete examples of how state legislatures have violated the rights of their own citizens under the Confederation.
ground_truth_signal: Must cite specific factional abuses from the Vices: paper money, installments of debts, occlusion of courts, making property legal tender. Rhode Island's paper money as paradigm case. 'All civilized societies are divided into different interests and factions, as they happen to be creditors or debtors — Rich or poor — husbandmen, merchants or manufacturers.' Debtors used majority power against creditors.
response:
The violations of citizen rights by state legislatures under our present Confederation present the most alarming evidence of the defects inherent in our system. These are not isolated incidents but systematic abuses that flow naturally from the structure of our governments, where unchecked majorities prey upon minorities with impunity.

The most notorious example is the conduct of Rhode Island, whose legislature has made property a legal tender and issued paper money with such reckless abandon that it has become a byword for legislative tyranny throughout the continent. When I consider whether an ordinary citizen or even an assembly-man of Rhode Island, in estimating the policy of paper money, ever considered or cared in what light the measure would be viewed in France or Holland, or even in Massachusetts or Connecticut, I find the answer instructive. It was a sufficient temptation to both that it was for their interest; it was a sufficient sanction to the latter that it was popular in the State, to the former that it was so in the neighbourhood.

This reveals the fundamental vice at work. All civilized societies are divided into different interests and factions, as they happen to be creditors or debtors — Rich or poor — husbandmen, merchants or manufacturers — members of different religious sects — followers of different political leaders — inhabitants of different districts — owners of different kinds of property. In republican Government the majority however composed, ultimately give the law. Whenever therefore an apparent interest or common passion unites a majority what is to restrain them from unjust violations of the rights and interests of the minority, or of individuals?

The paper money schemes represent precisely such a violation. Debtor majorities in several states have united to defraud their creditors through legislative artifice. They have made property a legal tender, established instalments of debts that effectively nullify contracts, and in some instances have gone so far as the occlusion of Courts to prevent creditors from seeking legal remedy. These measures operate as a species of legal robbery, sanctioned by the forms of republican government but destructive of its essential principles.

The effects extend beyond the immediate victims. As the Citizens of every State aggregately taken stand more or less in the relation of Creditors or debtors, to the Citizens of every other States, Acts of the debtor State in favor of debtors, affect the Creditor State, in the same manner, as they do its own citizens who are relatively creditors towards other citizens. This remark may be extended to foreign nations. Such violations of contract destroy confidence in our commercial intercourse and bring discredit upon the American name abroad.

The pattern repeats itself wherever factional majorities discover their power. In some states, religious minorities have found their property taxed for the support of establishments they conscientiously oppose. In others, particular trades or professions have been subjected to discriminatory regulations that favor competing interests possessed of greater political influence.

What renders these violations particularly pernicious is that they are accomplished through the ordinary processes of legislation. The forms of republican government are observed while its substance is corrupted. Three motives only might restrain such majorities from injustice: first, a prudent regard to their own good as involved in the general and permanent good of the Community — but this consideration, although of decisive weight in itself, is found by experience to be too often unheeded; second, respect for character — but in a multitude its efficacy is diminished in proportion to the number which is to share the praise or the blame; third, religion — but individuals join without remorse in acts, against which their consciences would revolt if proposed to them under the like sanction, separately in their closets.

The remedy cannot be found in exhortation to virtue, for the causes of faction are sown in the nature of man. Rather, we must modify the structure of government itself. If an enlargement of the sphere is found to lessen the insecurity of private rights, it is not because the impulse of a common interest or passion is less predominant in this case with the majority; but because a common interest or passion is less apt to be felt and the requisite combinations less easy to be formed by a great than by a small number. The Society becomes broken into a greater variety of interests, of pursuits, of passions, which check each other, whilst those who may feel a common sentiment have less opportunity of communication and concert.

This is why the inconveniences of popular States contrary to the prevailing Theory, are in proportion not to the extent, but to the narrowness of their limits. The great desideratum in Government is such a modification of the Sovereignty as will render it sufficiently neutral between the different interests and factions, to control one part of the Society from invading the rights of another, and at the same time sufficiently controlled itself, from setting up an interest adverse to that of the whole Society.
